- [x] **Idempotent property:** $a \cdot a = a$ for all $a \in \text{GF}(2)$.
- [x] **Subtraction equals addition** in GF(2) because $-1 = 1$.
- [x] **Additive group is $\mathbb{Z}_2$** (cyclic of order 2, generated by 1).
- [x] **Multiplicative group is trivial** $\{1\}$ of order 1.
- [x] **Characteristic of GF(2) is 2** — the smallest $n$ with $n \cdot 1 = 0$.
- [x] **Polynomial ring** $\text{GF}(2)[x]$ is the foundation of cyclic codes, BCH codes, and CRCs.
- [x] **The "Freshman's Dream":** $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + b^2$ in any characteristic-2 field.
- [x] **Polynomial addition** is coefficient-wise mod 2; **multiplication** is distribute-then-reduce.
- [x] **GF(2) is a 1-dimensional vector space** over itself; $\text{GF}(2^m)$ is $m$-dimensional.
- [x] **Irreducible polynomials** over GF(2) of degree $m$ generate the extension field $\text{GF}(2^m)$.
- [x] **Total number of monic polynomials of degree exactly $m$** over GF(2) is $2^{m-1}$ (for $m \geq 1$).
- [x] **Engineering bridge:** Syndrome decoding, Hamming codes, LFSRs, CRC, AES MixColumns — *all* use binary/extension field arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive Elements, Irreducible and Primitive Polynomials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT744 - CODING THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primitive Elements, Irreducible and Primitive Polynomials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Primitive Elements

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Primitive Element):**
> Let $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ be a finite field of order $q^n$. An element $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ is called a **primitive element** if and only if its multiplicative order is exactly $q^n - 1$. In other words, $\alpha$ is a generator of the cyclic multiplicative group $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$.

Mathematically:
$$\alpha \text{ is primitive} \iff \text{ord}(\alpha) = q^n - 1 = \vert \mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*} \vert$$

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Clock Face of Order $q^n - 1$":**
Imagine a giant clock with $q^n - 1$ marked positions (hours). A primitive element $\alpha$ is like a "magic hand" that touches every single position on the clock exactly once before returning to its starting position. If $\alpha$ were *not* primitive, the hand would only sweep a fraction of the dial, skipping many positions entirely. The powers $\alpha^0, \alpha^1, \alpha^2, \dots, \alpha^{q^n-2}$ produce *every* nonzero element of the field, which is why primitive elements are the workhorses of cyclic codes and LFSR-based stream ciphers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Existence is guaranteed because $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$ is a **cyclic group**. The number of primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ is $\phi(q^n - 1)$, where $\phi$ is Euler's totient function.

---

### 1.2 Irreducible Polynomials over $\mathbb{F}_q$

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Irreducible Polynomial):**
> A non-constant polynomial $f(x) \in \mathbb{F}_q[x]$ is called **irreducible** over $\mathbb{F}_q$ if it cannot be expressed as a product of two non-constant polynomials in $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$. Equivalently, the only divisors of $f(x)$ are the constants and its associates $c \cdot f(x)$.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Atoms of the Polynomial World":**
Just as prime numbers cannot be broken into smaller whole-number factors, irreducible polynomials are the *prime atoms* of $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$. Every polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_q$ can be factored (uniquely, by the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic for polynomial rings) into a product of irreducible factors — these are the "primes" of the polynomial universe. A polynomial of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ is irreducible precisely when it has no roots in $\mathbb{F}_q$ (necessary but **not sufficient** for $n > 3$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Existence):** For every prime power $q$ and every $n \geq 1$, there exists at least one monic irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$.

---

### 1.3 Primitive Polynomials

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Primitive Polynomial):**
> A monic polynomial $p(x) \in \mathbb{F}_q[x]$ of degree $n$ is a **primitive polynomial** if and only if:
> 1. $p(x)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_q$, AND
> 2. The smallest positive integer $e$ such that $p(x) \mid (x^e - 1)$ is $e = q^n - 1$.
>
> Equivalently: a root $\alpha$ of $p(x)$ is a primitive element of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Master Recipe":**
A primitive polynomial is the *minimal recipe* that generates the entire multiplicative world of the extension field. It is irreducible (cannot be simplified into smaller recipes) yet its root is so powerful that iterating it produces every nonzero field element before cycling. In hardware, this is the polynomial used to build a **Maximum-Length LFSR** of period $2^n - 1$ — the gold standard for pseudo-random bit generation.

---

### 1.4 Hierarchy at a Glance

The set relationships among these three objects are:

$$\{\text{Primitive Polynomials}\} \subset \{\text{Irreducible Polynomials}\} \subset \mathbb{F}_q[x]$$

$$\{\text{Primitive Elements}\} \subset \{\text{Generators of } \mathbb{F}_{q^n}^*\} \subset \mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Order of a Polynomial

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Order of a Polynomial):**
> For a non-constant polynomial $p(x) \in \mathbb{F}_q[x]$ with $p(0) \neq 0$, the **order** of $p(x)$, denoted $\text{ord}(p)$, is the smallest positive integer $e$ such that:
> $$p(x) \mid (x^e - 1)$$

For an irreducible polynomial of degree $n$, $\text{ord}(p)$ always divides $q^n - 1$ (this is a corollary of the fact that roots live in $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^*$). If equality holds, the polynomial is **primitive**.

### 2.2 Core Theorems (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Theorem 2.1 (Primitive Polynomial ⇒ Primitive Element Root)**
- *Step 1:* Let $p(x)$ be a primitive polynomial of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$.
- *Step 2:* Since $p(x)$ is irreducible of degree $n$, its root $\alpha$ generates the field $\mathbb{F}_q(\alpha) \cong \mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.
- *Step 3:* Since $p(x) \mid (x^{q^n-1} - 1)$, we have $\alpha^{q^n-1} = 1$.
- *Step 4:* By minimality of order $q^n - 1$, $\text{ord}(\alpha) = q^n - 1$.
- *Conclusion:* $\alpha$ is a primitive element of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.

**Theorem 2.2 (Roots of a Primitive Polynomial are All Primitive)**
- *Why?* The roots of an irreducible polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_q$ are the Galois conjugates $\alpha, \alpha^q, \alpha^{q^2}, \dots, \alpha^{q^{n-1}}$.
- *How?* Because $\alpha$ is primitive, $\alpha^{q^i}$ has the same order $q^n - 1$ (since $(\alpha^{q^i})^m = 1 \iff \alpha^{q^i \cdot m} = 1 \iff q^n - 1 \mid q^i m \iff q^n - 1 \mid m$ when $\gcd(q^i, q^n-1) = 1$).
- *Conclusion:* All $n$ roots of a primitive polynomial are primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.

**Theorem 2.3 (Necessary Condition)**
- *Statement:* If $p(x)$ is a primitive polynomial of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$, then $p(0) \neq 0$.
- *Reason:* If $p(0) = 0$, then $x \mid p(x)$, contradicting irreducibility.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table — Memorise for KTU ESE**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Statement | Variables & Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Size of multiplicative group | $\vert \mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*} \vert = q^n - 1$ | Always |
| 2 | Order of a primitive polynomial | $\text{ord}(p) = q^n - 1$ | $p$ primitive of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ |
| 3 | Order divides group size | $\text{ord}(p) \mid (q^n - 1)$ | $p$ irreducible of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ |
| 4 | # primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ | $\phi(q^n - 1)$ | $\phi$ = Euler totient |
| 5 | # primitive polys of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ | $N_{\text{prim}}(n,q) = \dfrac{\phi(q^n - 1)}{n}$ | Monic, irreducible |
| 6 | # monic irreducible polys of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ | $N(n,q) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{d \,\mid\, n} \mu(d)\, q^{\,n/d}$ | $\mu$ = Möbius function |
| 7 | # LFSR sequences of max length | Equals $N_{\text{prim}}(n,q)$ | For degree-$n$ LFSR over $\mathbb{F}_q$ |
| 8 | Primitive polynomial ⇒ $p(0) \neq 0$ | Excludes factor $x$ | Necessary |
| 9 | Period of $p(x)$ | $\text{period} = \text{ord}(p)$ | For LFSR applications |
| 10 | Generator polynomial of cyclic code | $g(x) \mid (x^n - 1)$, degree $= n - k$ | Where $n$ is block length, $k$ dimension |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Primitive Polynomial |
|---|---|
| **Cryptography (AES, RSA, ECC)** | Primitive elements generate full multiplicative subgroups for discrete-log-based schemes |
| **Stream Ciphers / LFSRs** | Primitive polynomials yield maximal-period sequences (m-sequences) of period $2^n - 1$ |
| **CRC Codes** | Irreducible (often primitive) polynomials provide strong error-detection capabilities |
| **BCH / Reed-Solomon Codes** | Cyclic codes built using minimal polynomials of primitive elements |
| **Spread-Spectrum Communication (CDMA)** | m-sequences from primitive polynomials give low cross-correlation families |
| **Monte Carlo Simulation** | m-sequences serve as high-quality pseudo-random number sources |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Primitive Polynomials of Degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$

We want to count the number of monic irreducible polynomials $p(x) \in \mathbb{F}_q[x]$ of degree $n$ whose roots are primitive elements.

**Step 1 — Count primitive elements.**
The multiplicative group $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$ is cyclic of order $q^n - 1$. The number of generators of a cyclic group of order $N$ is $\phi(N)$. Hence:
$$\text{Number of primitive elements} = \phi(q^n - 1)$$

**Step 2 — Count primitive polynomials per primitive element.**
Each monic irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ has exactly $n$ roots in $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$, all Galois conjugates of one another. Since all $n$ roots of a primitive polynomial are primitive elements (by Theorem 2.2), each primitive polynomial of degree $n$ corresponds to exactly $n$ primitive elements.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**
$$N_{\text{prim}}(n, q) \;=\; \frac{\text{Total primitive elements of degree-}n \text{ fields}}{\text{Primitive elements per polynomial}} \;=\; \frac{\phi(q^n - 1)}{n}$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Verify $p(x) = x^3 + x + 1$ is Primitive over $\mathbb{F}_2$

We must show that $p(x)$ is irreducible AND has order $2^3 - 1 = 7$.

**Step A — Irreducibility Check**
- *Test roots in $\mathbb{F}_2$:* $p(0) = 1 \neq 0$, $p(1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \neq 0$. No roots in $\mathbb{F}_2$.
- *Cannot factor as product of irreducible cubics of lower degree:* Since $\deg p = 3$, the only possible non-trivial factorisation would be $(\text{linear})(\text{quadratic})$. No linear factor $\Rightarrow$ irreducible.

**Step B — Compute Powers of $\alpha$ in $\mathbb{F}_8$**
Working in $\mathbb{F}_2[x]/(x^3 + x + 1)$, let $\alpha$ satisfy $\alpha^3 = \alpha + 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha^0 &= 1 \\
\alpha^1 &= \alpha \\
\alpha^2 &= \alpha^2 \\
\alpha^3 &= \alpha + 1 \\
\alpha^4 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^3 = \alpha(\alpha + 1) = \alpha^2 + \alpha \\
\alpha^5 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^4 = \alpha^3 + \alpha^2 = \alpha^2 + \alpha + 1 \\
\alpha^6 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^5 = \alpha^3 + \alpha^2 + \alpha = \alpha^2 + 1 \\
\alpha^7 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^6 = \alpha^3 + \alpha = (\alpha + 1) + \alpha = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step C — Conclusion**
The smallest positive $e$ with $\alpha^e = 1$ is $e = 7 = 2^3 - 1$. Therefore $\text{ord}(x^3 + x + 1) = 7$, confirming that $x^3 + x + 1$ is **primitive** over $\mathbb{F}_2$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> Using $N_{\text{prim}}(3,2) = \phi(7)/3 = 6/3 = 2$, so the only primitive polynomials of degree 3 over $\mathbb{F}_2$ are $x^3 + x + 1$ and $x^3 + x^2 + 1$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Primitive Polynomials of Degree 2 over $\mathbb{F}_3$

Apply $N_{\text{prim}}(2,3) = \phi(3^2 - 1)/2 = \phi(8)/2 = 4/2 = 2$. So we expect **2** primitive polynomials.

**Step 1 — List monic irreducible polynomials of degree 2 over $\mathbb{F}_3$.**
Test candidates: $x^2 + c_1 x + c_0$ with $c_0 \neq 0$ and no roots in $\mathbb{F}_3$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x^2 + 1 &: & 0^2+1 = 1,\ 1^2+1 = 2,\ 2^2+1 = 2 \;\;(\text{in } \mathbb{F}_3)\\
& & \Rightarrow \text{no roots. Irreducible.}\\
x^2 + x + 2 &: & 0+0+2=2,\ 1+1+2=1,\ 4+2+2=2 \Rightarrow \text{no roots. Irreducible.}\\
x^2 + 2x + 2 &: & 0+0+2=2,\ 1+2+2=2,\ 4+4+2=1 \Rightarrow \text{no roots. Irreducible.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute orders in $\mathbb{F}_9$.**
Let $\alpha$ be a root of $x^2 + 1$, so $\alpha^2 = -1 = 2 \pmod 3$.
- $\alpha^2 = 2$, $\alpha^4 = 2^2 = 4 = 1$. Order = 4 $\neq 8$. Not primitive.

Let $\beta$ be a root of $x^2 + x + 2$, so $\beta^2 = -\beta - 2 = 2\beta + 1$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta^2 &= 2\beta + 1 \\
\beta^3 &= \beta(2\beta + 1) = 2\beta^2 + \beta = 2(2\beta + 1) + \beta = 5\beta + 2 = 2\beta + 2 \\
\beta^4 &= \beta(2\beta + 2) = 2\beta^2 + 2\beta = 2(2\beta + 1) + 2\beta = 6\beta + 2 = 2 \\
\beta^5 &= 2\beta \\
\beta^6 &= 2\beta^2 = 2(2\beta + 1) = 4\beta + 2 = \beta + 2 \\
\beta^7 &= \beta(\beta + 2) = \beta^2 + 2\beta = 2\beta + 1 + 2\beta = 4\beta + 1 = \beta + 1 \\
\beta^8 &= \beta(\beta + 1) = \beta^2 + \beta = 2\beta + 1 + \beta = 3\beta + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

Order of $\beta$ is $8 = 3^2 - 1$. **Primitive!**

By symmetry (and a similar computation), $x^2 + 2x + 2$ is also primitive.

**Step 3 — Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{\text{Primitive polynomials of degree 2 over } \mathbb{F}_3 : \quad x^2 + x + 2 \quad \text{and} \quad x^2 + 2x + 2}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Gauss's Formula for $N(n, q)$

The number of monic irreducible polynomials of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ is:
$$N(n, q) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d)\, q^{\,n/d}$$

This follows from generating-function techniques applied to the partition of monic polynomials by irreducible factorisation and Möbius inversion on the divisor lattice of $n$. KTU students are expected to **quote** the result and **apply** it — not to reprove it in exams.

**Quick Verification:** $N(1, q) = \frac{1}{1}(\mu(1)q + 0) = q$, correctly giving $x, x-1, x-2, \dots, x-(q-1)$ as the $q$ linear monic irreducibles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Polynomial Ring F_q of x"] --> B["Monic Irreducible Polynomials"]
    A --> C["Reducible Polynomials"]
    B --> D["Primitive Polynomials"]
    B --> E["Non Primitive Irreducible"]
    D --> F["Roots alpha in F_q power n"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Multiplicative Group F_q_n star"]
    G --> H["Order q^n minus 1"]
    D --> H
    F --> I["Primitive Elements"]
    F --> J["Non Primitive Elements"]
    I --> K["Count phi of q^n minus 1"]
    D --> L["Count phi of q^n minus 1 over n"]
    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style H fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style I fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6
```

**How to read this diagram:** Start at the top with $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$; every polynomial is either reducible or monic irreducible. Among monic irreducibles, only the **primitive** ones have roots of full order $q^n - 1$. The count formulas are annotated on the right.

### 4.2 Block Architecture — LFSR Built from a Primitive Polynomial

```mermaid
graph LR
    CLK["Clock Signal"] --> REG["n-bit Shift Register"]
    REG --> FB["Feedback Taps"]
    FB --> XOR["XOR Sum over F_q"]
    XOR --> REG
    REG --> OUT["Output Bit Stream"]
    P["Primitive Polynomial p of x"] -. defines .-> FB
    P -. ensures .-> PER["Period equals q power n minus 1"]
    style CLK fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style P fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style PER fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
```

**Interpretation:** The primitive polynomial $p(x)$ uniquely specifies *which* register positions are tapped and XOR-summed back into the input. The maximal-period property $2^n - 1$ (binary case) is what makes this polynomial "primitive" — the LFSR cycles through *every* nonzero state before repeating.

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Polynomial Orders

```mermaid
graph TD
    O1["Order 1 trivial x minus 1"]
    O3["Order 3 examples x squared plus x plus 1"]
    O7["Order 7 primitive x cubed plus x plus 1"]
    O15["Order 15 primitive x^4 plus x plus 1 over F_2"]
    O31["Order 31 primitive degree 5 polys over F_2"]
    F2n1["Set of all orders dividing 2^n minus 1"]
    F2n1 --> O1
    F2n1 --> O3
    F2n1 --> O7
    F2n1 --> O15
    F2n1 --> O31
    style O7 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style O15 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style O31 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
    style F2n1 fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6
```

**Interpretation:** For binary polynomials of degree $n$, valid orders are divisors of $2^n - 1$. *Primitive* polynomials sit at the **maximum** order $2^n - 1$. The green nodes are the primitive ones.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a primitive polynomial. State any two of its properties.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A monic polynomial $p(x) \in \mathbb{F}_q[x]$ of degree $n$ is called a **primitive polynomial** if it is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_q$ and the smallest positive integer $e$ with $p(x) \mid (x^e - 1)$ equals $q^n - 1$.

*Properties (any two):*
1. Every root of a primitive polynomial is a primitive element of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.
2. The number of primitive polynomials of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\phi(q^n - 1)/n$.
3. The LFSR defined by a primitive polynomial generates a maximal-length sequence of period $q^n - 1$.
4. $p(0) \neq 0$ (otherwise $x \mid p$, contradicting irreducibility).

---

**Q2. Distinguish between an irreducible polynomial and a primitive polynomial with a suitable example.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Irreducible Polynomial | Primitive Polynomial |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Cannot be factored over $\mathbb{F}_q$ | Irreducible **AND** order $= q^n - 1$ |
| Subset of | $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$ | Monic irreducible polynomials (proper subset) |
| Example over $\mathbb{F}_2$ | $x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + x + 1$ (irreducible but order 5) | $x^3 + x + 1$ (order 7) |

The polynomial $x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + x + 1$ is irreducible but **not** primitive because its order is $5 \neq 15 = 2^4 - 1$. In contrast, $x^3 + x + 1$ has order $7 = 2^3 - 1$, making it primitive.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper Pattern, May 2025 Expected Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define a primitive element of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$. Prove that the number of primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ is $\phi(q^n - 1)$, where $\phi$ denotes Euler's totient function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Verify whether $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1$ is a primitive polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_2$. Justify each step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

*Definition:* An element $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ is a primitive element if $\text{ord}(\alpha) = q^n - 1$, i.e., $\alpha$ generates the multiplicative group $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$.

*Proof:* Since $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$ is a finite subgroup of the multiplicative group of a field, it is cyclic. Let $g$ be a generator of this cyclic group of order $N = q^n - 1$. A general element is $g^k$ for some integer $k$. Its order is:
$$\text{ord}(g^k) = \frac{N}{\gcd(N, k)}$$

For $g^k$ to be primitive, we need $\text{ord}(g^k) = N$, which holds iff $\gcd(N, k) = 1$. The number of integers $k$ with $1 \leq k \leq N$ such that $\gcd(k, N) = 1$ is precisely $\phi(N) = \phi(q^n - 1)$. Hence the number of primitive elements is $\phi(q^n - 1)$. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Irreducibility check.**
Test roots in $\mathbb{F}_2$: $p(0) = 1$, $p(1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \neq 0$. No linear factor.

For degree 4 over $\mathbb{F}_2$, the only irreducible quadratic is $x^2 + x + 1$. Check:
$$(x^2 + x + 1)^2 = x^4 + x^2 + 1 \neq x^4 + x + 1$$
So $p(x)$ does not factor into quadratics either. Hence $p(x)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the order.**
Work in $\mathbb{F}_2[x]/(x^4 + x + 1)$ with $\alpha^4 = \alpha + 1$. Required: smallest $e$ with $\alpha^e = 1$, expecting $e = 15$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha^0 &= 1 \\
\alpha^1 &= \alpha \\
\alpha^2 &= \alpha^2 \\
\alpha^3 &= \alpha^3 \\
\alpha^4 &= \alpha + 1 \\
\alpha^5 &= \alpha^2 + \alpha \\
\alpha^6 &= \alpha^3 + \alpha^2 \\
\alpha^7 &= \alpha^4 + \alpha^3 = \alpha^3 + \alpha + 1 \\
\alpha^8 &= \alpha^4 + \alpha^2 + \alpha = \alpha^2 + 1 \\
\alpha^{15} &= \alpha^{4 \cdot 3 + 3} = (\alpha^4)^3 \cdot \alpha^3 = (\alpha + 1)^3 \cdot \alpha^3
\end{aligned}$$

Quick check of small divisors of 15 (3 and 5): $\alpha^3 \neq 1$ and $\alpha^5 = \alpha^2 + \alpha \neq 1$. So order is not 3 or 5. Since order must divide 15 and is neither 1, 3, nor 5, the order must be **15**.

**Conclusion:** $\text{ord}(x^4 + x + 1) = 15 = 2^4 - 1$, so $x^4 + x + 1$ is **primitive** over $\mathbb{F}_2$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** State and prove the formula for the number of primitive polynomials of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Determine all primitive polynomials of degree 2 over $\mathbb{F}_3$. Show all necessary computations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Statement:** The number of monic primitive polynomials of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ is
$$N_{\text{prim}}(n, q) = \frac{\phi(q^n - 1)}{n}$$

**Proof:** Every monic irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ has exactly $n$ distinct roots in $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$, namely $\alpha, \alpha^q, \alpha^{q^2}, \dots, \alpha^{q^{n-1}}$, all Galois conjugates. Since these $n$ roots share the same multiplicative order, either *all* are primitive or *none* are. Hence each primitive polynomial of degree $n$ accounts for exactly $n$ primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.

Conversely, every primitive element $\beta \in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ has a minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_q$ of degree $n$, and this minimal polynomial is irreducible with primitive root — i.e., a primitive polynomial. So primitive elements and primitive polynomials of degree $n$ are in bijection modulo the $n$-to-1 correspondence between elements and their minimal polynomial.

Total primitive elements = $\phi(q^n - 1)$ (by Question A(a)). Dividing by $n$ yields:
$$N_{\text{prim}}(n, q) = \frac{\phi(q^n - 1)}{n}. \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

Expected count: $N_{\text{prim}}(2, 3) = \phi(8)/2 = 4/2 = 2$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate all monic irreducible polynomials of degree 2 over $\mathbb{F}_3$.**
These are $x^2 + ax + b$ with $b \neq 0$ (else $x$ is a root) and no root in $\mathbb{F}_3$.

Test $b = 1$:
- $x^2 + 1$: $f(0)=1, f(1)=2, f(2)=2$. Irreducible.
- $x^2 + x + 1$: $f(1) = 1+1+1 = 0$ in $\mathbb{F}_3$. Root! Reducible.
- $x^2 + 2x + 1$: $f(1) = 1+2+1 = 1, f(2) = 4+4+1 = 0$. Root! Reducible.

Test $b = 2$:
- $x^2 + 2$: $f(0)=2, f(1)=0$. Root! Reducible.
- $x^2 + x + 2$: $f(0)=2, f(1)=1, f(2)=4+2+2=2$. Irreducible.
- $x^2 + 2x + 2$: $f(0)=2, f(1)=1, f(2)=4+4+2=1$. Irreducible.

The three monic irreducible polynomials of degree 2 over $\mathbb{F}_3$ are:
$$p_1(x) = x^2 + 1, \quad p_2(x) = x^2 + x + 2, \quad p_3(x) = x^2 + 2x + 2.$$

**Step 2 — Check orders.**

Let $\alpha$ be a root of $p_1$: $\alpha^2 = -1 = 2$. Then $\alpha^4 = 4 = 1$. Order = 4 $\neq 8$. **Not primitive.**

Let $\beta$ be a root of $p_2$: $\beta^2 = -\beta - 2 = 2\beta + 1$. Compute:
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta^2 &= 2\beta + 1 \\
\beta^4 &= (2\beta+1)^2 = 4\beta^2 + 4\beta + 1 = \beta^2 + \beta + 1 \\
&= (2\beta+1) + \beta + 1 = 3\beta + 2 = 2 \\
\beta^8 &= 2^2 = 4 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

Check $\beta^2 = 2\beta + 1 \neq 1$ and $\beta^4 = 2 \neq 1$, so order is exactly 8. **Primitive!**

By similar computation (or using the conjugate $\beta^{1/2}$ symmetry), $p_3(x) = x^2 + 2x + 2$ is also primitive.

**Final Answer:** The primitive polynomials of degree 2 over $\mathbb{F}_3$ are:
$$\boxed{x^2 + x + 2 \quad \text{and} \quad x^2 + 2x + 2.}$$

This matches the predicted count of 2. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "irreducible" requirement.** Many students call *any* polynomial of order $q^n - 1$ "primitive" — but reducible polynomials are excluded. *Always check irreducibility first.*
> 2. **Confusing polynomial order with element order.** They are linked (the polynomial's order equals the order of any of its roots) but are not the *same* object. State both clearly.
> 3. **Wrong totient computation.** $\phi(15) = 8$ (numbers coprime to 15 in $\{1,\dots,14\}$ are $1,2,4,7,8,11,13,14$). Many students write $\phi(15) = 7$. *Memorise totients of small numbers.*
> 4. **Forgetting to verify small divisors of $q^n - 1$.** When proving a polynomial is primitive, you must show it does **not** have any smaller order dividing $q^n - 1$ (e.g., 3 or 5 in the binary-degree-4 case).
> 5. **Careless arithmetic in $\mathbb{F}_2$ vs $\mathbb{Z}$.** Remember $-1 = 1$ in $\mathbb{F}_2$! Always reduce coefficients mod 2.
> 6. **Applying Euler's theorem without verifying $\gcd$.** Euler's theorem requires $\gcd(a, N) = 1$; in fields this is automatic, but the formula application needs care.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1**

- **Primitive Element:** $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{q^n}$ with multiplicative order $q^n - 1$. It generates the cyclic group $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$.
- **Irreducible Polynomial:** Has no non-trivial factorisation over $\mathbb{F}_q$. Analogous to primes in $\mathbb{Z}$.
- **Primitive Polynomial:** Monic irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ whose root is a primitive element of $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$.
- **Order of Polynomial:** Smallest $e > 0$ such that $p(x) \mid (x^e - 1)$.
- **Key Numbers to Memorise:**
  * $\phi(3) = 2$, $\phi(7) = 6$, $\phi(15) = 8$, $\phi(31) = 30$, $\phi(63) = 36$, $\phi(127) = 126$, $\phi(255) = 128$.
  * $2^n - 1$: prime for $n = 2, 3, 5, 7, 13, 17, 19, 31, 61, \dots$ (Mersenne primes).
- **Counting Formulas:**
  * Primitive polynomials: $\phi(q^n - 1)/n$.
  * All monic irreducible polynomials: $(1/n)\sum_{d \mid n} \mu(d) q^{n/d}$.
- **Hierarchy:** Primitive ⊂ Irreducible ⊂ $\mathbb{F}_q[x]$. Primitive element ⊂ $\mathbb{F}_{q^n}^{\,*}$.
- **All Galois conjugates of a primitive root are primitive.**
- **A primitive polynomial has $p(0) \neq 0$.**
- **Application Hook:** Primitive polynomials drive maximal-length LFSRs (period $2^n - 1$) — these are the heart of stream ciphers and CRC design.
- **Standard Test Procedure:** (1) Verify irreducibility; (2) compute orders of powers of root $\alpha$; (3) confirm smallest $e$ with $\alpha^e = 1$ equals $q^n - 1$.
- **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** $x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + x + 1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$ is irreducible but **NOT** primitive (order 5, not 15).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conjugate Elements and Minimal Polynomials - Field Extension - Construction of Finite Fields from Polynomial Rings, Vector Spaces, Subspaces and Dual Spaces, Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT744 - CODING THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conjugate Elements and Minimal Polynomials - Field Extension - Construction of Finite Fields from Polynomial Rings, Vector Spaces, Subspaces and Dual Spaces, Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conjugate Elements

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

Let $F$ be a field and let $K$ be an extension field of $F$. Two elements $\alpha, \beta \in K$ are called **conjugate over $F$** if they are roots of the same minimal polynomial $m_{\alpha}(x) = m_{\beta}(x) \in F[x]$ over the base field $F$.

Equivalently, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are $F$-conjugates if and only if there exists a field isomorphism $\sigma : F(\alpha) \to F(\beta)$ fixing $F$ pointwise such that $\sigma(\alpha) = \beta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimal Polynomial Definition (KTU Board Standard):** Let $F$ be a field and $\alpha$ be algebraic over $F$. The **minimal polynomial** of $\alpha$ over $F$, denoted $m_{\alpha,F}(x)$ (or simply $m_{\alpha}(x)$ when context is clear), is the monic polynomial of least degree in $F[x]$ such that $m_{\alpha}(\alpha) = 0$. It is always irreducible over $F$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of conjugate elements as **"genetic twins" born from the same parent polynomial**. Just as identical twins share the same DNA (genetic code) but can live in different places, conjugate elements share the same "algebraic DNA" (their minimal polynomial) but exist as distinct roots in the larger extension field.

For example, in the complex plane, the numbers $i$ and $-i$ are conjugate over $\mathbb{R}$ because both are roots of the minimal polynomial $x^2 + 1 \in \mathbb{R}[x]$. They are "twins" of the same algebraic family, but they live at different geometric locations ($i$ points "up" and $-i$ points "down" in $\Argand$ space).

---

## 1.2 Field Extension

### Formal Definition

A **field extension** $K/F$ is a pair of fields where $F \subseteq K$ and the field operations of $K$ restrict to those of $F$ on the subset $F$. The field $F$ is called the **base field** and $K$ the **extension field**.

We write $[K : F]$ to denote the **degree of the extension**, which is the dimension of $K$ when viewed as a vector space over $F$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine $\mathbb{Q}$ (rationals) as a small village. The complex numbers $\mathbb{C}$ form a vast country that *contains* the village. The roads and laws (operations) of the village still work inside the country, but the country has many more inhabitants (elements like $\sqrt{2}, i, \pi$) that the village does not have. The "size" of the country relative to the village is measured by the **extension degree** $[K : F]$.

---

## 1.3 Construction of Finite Fields from Polynomial Rings

### Formal Definition

A **finite field** (or Galois field) of order $p^n$ is constructed as the quotient ring:

$$
\mathbb{F}_{p^n} \;\cong\; \mathbb{F}_p[x] \,/\, \langle p(x) \rangle
$$

where $p$ is a prime, $n \geq 1$ is a positive integer, and $p(x)$ is a monic irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ in $\mathbb{F}_p[x]$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of building a finite field as constructing a clock with $p^n$ hours. The prime field $\mathbb{F}_p$ provides the "modular arithmetic" rules (the clock face), and the irreducible polynomial $p(x)$ dictates how the "unknown" element $\alpha$ (the new hour hand) behaves. The result is a complete, self-consistent arithmetic system with exactly $p^n$ elements.

---

## 1.4 Vector Spaces, Subspaces, and Dual Spaces

### Formal Definitions

Let $F$ be a field (frequently a finite field in coding theory). A **vector space** $V$ over $F$ is an abelian group $(V, +)$ together with a scalar multiplication $F \times V \to V$ satisfying the standard eight axioms.

A **subspace** $W$ of $V$ is a nonempty subset of $V$ that is itself a vector space under the inherited operations. Equivalently, $W \subseteq V$ is a subspace if and only if it is closed under addition and scalar multiplication:

$$
\forall u, v \in W,\ \forall c \in F:\quad u + v \in W \text{ and } c \cdot u \in W
$$

The **dual space** $V^*$ of $V$ is the set of all linear functionals (linear maps from $V$ to the base field $F$):

$$
V^* \;=\; \text{Hom}_F(V, F) \;=\; \{ \varphi : V \to F \mid \varphi \text{ is } F\text{-linear} \}
$$

The dual space $V^*$ is itself a vector space over $F$ with dimension $\dim_F(V^*) = \dim_F(V)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Dual Spaces Matter in Coding Theory:** In linear block codes, the dual of a code $C$ is the set of all vectors orthogonal to every codeword. This is the foundation of **dual codes** and **syndrome decoding**.

---

## 1.5 Matrices over Finite Fields

### Formal Definition

An $m \times n$ **matrix over a field $F$** is a rectangular array

$$
A \;=\; (a_{ij})_{1 \leq i \leq m,\, 1 \leq j \leq n}, \quad a_{ij} \in F
$$

The set of all $m \times n$ matrices over $F$ is denoted $M_{m \times n}(F)$. Square matrices $M_n(F)$ form an $F$-algebra under matrix addition and multiplication.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

A matrix is like a **translator** between two vector spaces. Given an input vector $\mathbf{x}$, the matrix $A$ produces an output $A\mathbf{x}$ via a fixed linear recipe. In coding theory, the **generator matrix** $G$ encodes messages into codewords, while the **parity-check matrix** $H$ detects errors by checking orthogonality: $H\mathbf{c}^T = \mathbf{0}$ for every valid codeword $\mathbf{c}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every finite field construction $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ yields a natural vector space of dimension $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$. This single observation is the bridge between **abstract algebra** and the **vector-space framework** used in linear coding theory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Minimal Polynomials

Let $\alpha$ be an algebraic element over a field $F$. Then the minimal polynomial $m_{\alpha}(x) \in F[x]$ satisfies the following properties:

1. **Uniqueness:** $m_{\alpha}(x)$ is the **unique** monic polynomial of minimum degree in $F[x]$ with $\alpha$ as a root.
2. **Irreducibility:** $m_{\alpha}(x)$ is **irreducible** over $F$. If it factored as $m_{\alpha}(x) = g(x)h(x)$, then $g(\alpha) = 0$ or $h(\alpha) = 0$, contradicting minimality of degree.
3. **Divisibility:** If $f(x) \in F[x]$ and $f(\alpha) = 0$, then $m_{\alpha}(x)$ **divides** $f(x)$ in $F[x]$.
4. **Degree Bound:** $\deg(m_{\alpha}) = [F(\alpha) : F]$, i.e., the degree of the minimal polynomial equals the degree of the simple extension $F(\alpha)/F$.
5. **Root Identification:** All conjugates of $\alpha$ over $F$ are exactly the distinct roots of $m_{\alpha}(x)$ in any algebraic closure $\overline{F}$.

### Why and How Behind These Properties

- **Why unique?** Two distinct monic polynomials of minimum degree both having $\alpha$ as a root would have a nonzero difference with $\alpha$ as a root, again contradicting minimality.
- **Why irreducible?** Suppose $m_{\alpha}(x) = g(x)h(x)$ with $\deg g, \deg h < \deg m_{\alpha}$. Then $0 = m_{\alpha}(\alpha) = g(\alpha)h(\alpha)$ forces one factor to vanish at $\alpha$, contradicting minimal degree.
- **Why degree equals extension degree?** The set $\{1, \alpha, \alpha^2, \ldots, \alpha^{n-1}\}$ where $n = \deg m_{\alpha}$ forms a basis for $F(\alpha)$ over $F$.

---

## 2.2 Conjugates in Finite Fields $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$

Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ and let $m_{\alpha}(x) \in \mathbb{F}_p[x]$ be its minimal polynomial. The **Frobenius automorphism** $\phi : x \mapsto x^p$ fixes $\mathbb{F}_p$ pointwise and permutes the roots of $m_{\alpha}(x)$. Therefore, the distinct conjugates of $\alpha$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$ are:

$$
\alpha,\ \alpha^p,\ \alpha^{p^2},\ \alpha^{p^3},\ \ldots, \alpha^{p^{n-1}}
$$

until repetition occurs. The number of distinct conjugates equals $\deg m_{\alpha}(x)$.

### The Order-Relating Formula

If $d$ is the multiplicative order of $\alpha$ in $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}^\times$, and $e$ is the order of $p$ modulo $d$, then:

$$
\deg m_{\alpha}(x) \;=\; e
$$

where $e$ is the smallest positive integer such that $p^e \equiv 1 \pmod{d}$.

---

## 2.3 Field Extension Theorems

### Tower Law (Multiplicative Property of Extension Degree)

If $F \subseteq K \subseteq L$ are fields, then:

$$
[L : F] \;=\; [L : K] \cdot [K : F]
$$

### Existence Theorem for Simple Extensions

For any irreducible polynomial $p(x) \in F[x]$ of degree $n$, there exists a field extension $K/F$ of degree $n$ such that $K \cong F[x]/\langle p(x) \rangle$ and $K = F(\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is a root of $p(x)$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Minimal polynomial of $\alpha$ | $m_{\alpha}(x)$ = monic irreducible polynomial of least degree in $F[x]$ with $m_{\alpha}(\alpha) = 0$ |
| 2 | Conjugates of $\alpha$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$ in $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ | $\{\alpha, \alpha^p, \alpha^{p^2}, \ldots, \alpha^{p^{n-1}}\}$ |
| 3 | Number of conjugates | $\deg(m_{\alpha}(x))$ |
| 4 | Degree of simple extension | $[F(\alpha) : F] = \deg(m_{\alpha}(x))$ |
| 5 | Tower Law | $[L : F] = [L : K] \cdot [K : F]$ |
| 6 | Order of $\alpha$ vs degree of minimal poly | $\deg m_{\alpha} = $ smallest $e$ with $\alpha^{p^e} = \alpha$ |
| 7 | Finite field order | $\vert \mathbb{F}_{p^n} \vert = p^n$ |
| 8 | Finite field construction | $\mathbb{F}_{p^n} \cong \mathbb{F}_p[x] / \langle p(x) \rangle$, $p(x)$ irreducible of degree $n$ |
| 9 | Multiplicative group | $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}^\times$ is cyclic of order $p^n - 1$ |
| 10 | Subspace test | $W \subseteq V$ is a subspace iff $u + cv \in W$ for all $u, v \in W, c \in F$ |
| 11 | Dual space dimension | $\dim_F V^* = \dim_F V$ |
| 12 | Rank-Nullity Theorem | $\text{rank}(A) + \text{nullity}(A) = n$ for $A : F^n \to F^m$ |
| 13 | Generator matrix property | $C = \{uG : u \in \mathbb{F}_q^k\}$ is a $[n, k]$ linear code |
| 14 | Parity-check property | $H c^T = 0$ for all $c \in C$, where $HG^T = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert \mathbb{F}_{p^n} \vert$ uses the standard "order" notation. The character has been deliberately avoided inside table rows to prevent markdown rendering errors.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cryptography:** AES, RSA, and elliptic curve cryptography rely on arithmetic in $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}$ and $\mathbb{F}_p$ where $p$ is a large prime. Conjugate computations appear in trace and norm functions used for cryptographic hashing.
- **Error-Correcting Codes:** Reed-Solomon codes (used in QR codes, Blu-ray discs, deep-space communication) are constructed over finite fields. Generator and parity-check matrices are matrix representations of the encoding/decoding operations.
- **Signal Processing:** Discrete Fourier Transforms over finite fields (Number Theoretic Transforms) are used in efficient convolution and correlation in coding applications.
- **Wireless Communication:** Convolutional codes, turbo codes, and LDPC codes use matrix algebra over finite fields for encoding and decoding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conjugates of $\alpha$ in $\mathbb{F}_{2^4}$ via the Polynomial $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1$

**Setup:** Let $p = 2$ and let $\alpha$ be a root of the irreducible polynomial $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1 \in \mathbb{F}_2[x]$. The field $\mathbb{F}_{2^4}$ is constructed as $\mathbb{F}_2[x]/\langle x^4 + x + 1 \rangle$, so $\alpha^4 = \alpha + 1$ inside this field.

**Step 1:** Identify the minimal polynomial of $\alpha$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

Since $p(x)$ is irreducible of degree 4 over $\mathbb{F}_2$, and $\alpha$ is a root of $p(x)$, the minimal polynomial of $\alpha$ is exactly $p(x)$:

$$
m_{\alpha}(x) \;=\; x^4 + x + 1
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Frobenius map $\phi(\beta) = \beta^2$ to generate conjugates.

$$
\alpha^2 \quad \text{(distinct from } \alpha \text{)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\alpha^4$ using the field relation $\alpha^4 = \alpha + 1$.

$$
\alpha^4 \;=\; \alpha + 1
$$

This is also distinct from $\alpha$ and $\alpha^2$ in general (we verify non-equality in a moment).

**Step 4:** Compute $\alpha^8$.

$$
\alpha^8 \;=\; (\alpha^4)^2 \;=\; (\alpha + 1)^2 \;=\; \alpha^2 + 2\alpha + 1 \;=\; \alpha^2 + 1
$$

since $2\alpha = 0$ in characteristic 2.

**Step 5:** Check whether the orbit has closed.

The minimal polynomial has degree 4, so we expect exactly 4 distinct conjugates. Let us check if $\alpha^{16} = \alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha^{16} \;=\; (\alpha^8)^2 \;=\; (\alpha^2 + 1)^2 \;=\; \alpha^4 + 1 \;=\; (\alpha + 1) + 1 \;=\; \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

since $1 + 1 = 0$ in $\mathbb{F}_2$. So the orbit closes after exactly 4 elements.

**Step 6:** The four distinct conjugates of $\alpha$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$ are:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha,\ \alpha^2,\ \alpha^4 = \alpha + 1,\ \alpha^8 = \alpha^2 + 1\;}
$$

The minimal polynomial, with these as roots, factors in $\mathbb{F}_2[x]$ only trivially because the polynomial is irreducible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Constructing $\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$ Using $p(x) = x^3 + x + 1$

**Step 1:** Verify irreducibility of $x^3 + x + 1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

A degree-3 polynomial over a field is irreducible iff it has no root in the field. Test:

$$
p(0) = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1 \neq 0, \qquad p(1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \neq 0
$$

Both are nonzero, so $x^3 + x + 1$ has no root in $\mathbb{F}_2$ and is therefore **irreducible**.

**Step 2:** Form the quotient ring $R = \mathbb{F}_2[x] / \langle x^3 + x + 1 \rangle$.

Every element of $R$ has a unique representative of the form:

$$
a_0 + a_1 \alpha + a_2 \alpha^2, \quad a_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

where $\alpha = x + \langle x^3 + x + 1 \rangle$ is the image of $x$, satisfying $\alpha^3 = \alpha + 1$.

**Step 3:** Enumerate the $2^3 = 8$ elements of $\mathbb{F}_2[\alpha]$:

$$
\{0,\ 1,\ \alpha,\ \alpha+1,\ \alpha^2,\ \alpha^2+1,\ \alpha^2+\alpha,\ \alpha^2+\alpha+1\}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify that $\alpha$ is a **primitive element** by computing successive powers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha^0 &= 1 \\
\alpha^1 &= \alpha \\
\alpha^2 &= \alpha^2 \\
\alpha^3 &= \alpha + 1 \\
\alpha^4 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^3 = \alpha(\alpha + 1) = \alpha^2 + \alpha \\
\alpha^5 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^4 = \alpha^3 + \alpha^2 = (\alpha + 1) + \alpha^2 = \alpha^2 + \alpha + 1 \\
\alpha^6 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^5 = \alpha^3 + \alpha^2 + \alpha = (\alpha + 1) + \alpha^2 + \alpha = \alpha^2 + 1 \\
\alpha^7 &= \alpha \cdot \alpha^6 = \alpha^3 + \alpha = (\alpha + 1) + \alpha = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $\alpha^7 = 1$ and the multiplicative group has order $2^3 - 1 = 7$ (which is prime), $\alpha$ generates the entire group:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{F}_{2^3}^\times \;=\; \langle \alpha \rangle \;=\; \{1, \alpha, \alpha^2, \alpha^3, \alpha^4, \alpha^5, \alpha^6\}\;}
$$

So $\alpha$ is a primitive element of $\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Conjugates of $\alpha^3$ in $\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$

We use the **order-degree** relationship. The element $\beta = \alpha^3$ has multiplicative order $7 / \gcd(3, 7) = 7$ (since $\gcd(3,7) = 1$), so $\beta$ is also a primitive element.

**Step 1:** Compute the order of $p = 2$ modulo $d = 7$.

We need the smallest $e$ with $2^e \equiv 1 \pmod 7$:

$$
2^1 = 2,\quad 2^2 = 4,\quad 2^3 = 8 \equiv 1 \pmod 7
$$

So $e = 3$.

**Step 2:** Therefore $\deg m_{\beta}(x) = 3$, meaning $\beta$ has exactly 3 distinct conjugates over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

**Step 3:** Compute the conjugates explicitly:

$$
\beta = \alpha^3 = \alpha + 1
$$

$$
\beta^2 = (\alpha + 1)^2 = \alpha^2 + 1
$$

$$
\beta^4 = (\beta^2)^2 = (\alpha^2 + 1)^2 = \alpha^4 + 1 = (\alpha^2 + \alpha) + 1 = \alpha^2 + \alpha + 1
$$

**Step 4:** Since $\deg m_{\beta} = 3$ and the conjugates are distinct, we read off the minimal polynomial:

$$
m_{\beta}(x) \;=\; (x - \beta)(x - \beta^2)(x - \beta^4)
$$

After expanding (which is a routine but lengthy calculation), and using the fact that the result must lie in $\mathbb{F}_2[x]$, one obtains the irreducible polynomial $x^3 + x + 1$ (the same one used to construct the field). This is no accident — by the theory of cyclotomic polynomials, primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$ share the same minimal polynomial.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Dual Space of a $[n, k]$ Linear Code

**Setup:** Let $C$ be a linear block code of length $n$ and dimension $k$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$. Then $C$ is a $k$-dimensional subspace of the $n$-dimensional vector space $V = \mathbb{F}_q^n$.

**Step 1:** Define the standard inner product.

For $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{F}_q^n$:

$$
\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i v_i
$$

**Step 2:** Define the dual code.

$$
C^\perp \;=\; \{ \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{F}_q^n \mid \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0 \text{ for all } \mathbf{u} \in C \}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify $C^\perp$ is a subspace.

- $0 \in C^\perp$ since $\langle \mathbf{u}, 0 \rangle = 0$.
- If $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2 \in C^\perp$, then $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2 \rangle = \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}_1 \rangle + \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}_2 \rangle = 0$, so $\mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2 \in C^\perp$.
- If $\mathbf{v} \in C^\perp$ and $c \in \mathbb{F}_q$, then $\langle \mathbf{u}, c\mathbf{v} \rangle = c \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$, so $c\mathbf{v} \in C^\perp$.

**Step 4:** Compute the dimension.

By the rank-nullity theorem applied to the linear map $C \to \mathbb{F}_q^n$ given by $\mathbf{u} \mapsto (\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}_1 \rangle, \ldots, \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}_n \rangle)$ (or directly by dimension counting):

$$
\dim C + \dim C^\perp \;=\; n
$$

So $\dim C^\perp = n - k$, and $C^\perp$ is an $[n, n-k]$ linear code.

**Step 5:** Relate to matrices.

If $G$ is a generator matrix of $C$ (so rows of $G$ span $C$), then a parity-check matrix $H$ of $C$ has rows that span $C^\perp$. The fundamental relation is:

$$
H G^T \;=\; 0
$$

Equivalently, every codeword $\mathbf{c} \in C$ satisfies:

$$
H \mathbf{c}^T \;=\; \mathbf{0}
$$

This is the foundation of **syndrome decoding**: for a received vector $\mathbf{r}$, the syndrome $H\mathbf{r}^T$ depends only on the error pattern $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{c}$ and is used to identify (and correct) errors.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Algebraic Hierarchy from Field to Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Field F] --> B[Polynomial Ring F x]
    B --> C[Irreducible Polynomial p x of degree n]
    C --> D[Quotient Ring F x mod p x]
    D --> E[Finite Field F sub p sub n]
    E --> F[Vector Space V over F sub p of dimension n]
    F --> G[Subspace C of dimension k]
    G --> H[Dual Code C perp of dimension n-k]
    H --> I[Parity Check Matrix H]
    G --> J[Generator Matrix G]
    I --> K[Syndrome Decoding]
    J --> K
    E --> L[Conjugate Elements alpha, alpha p, alpha p2 ...]
    L --> M[Minimal Polynomial m alpha x]
    M --> N[Conjugacy Class]
```

## 4.2 Frobenius Orbit Diagram for Conjugates in $\mathbb{F}_{2^4}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[alpha] -- phi maps to --> A1[alpha squared]
    A1 -- phi maps to --> A2[alpha fourth equals alpha plus 1]
    A2 -- phi maps to --> A3[alpha eighth equals alpha squared plus 1]
    A3 -- phi maps to --> A0
    A0 -. degree of minimal poly .-> P0[deg = 4]
    A1 -. degree of minimal poly .-> P0
    A2 -. degree of minimal poly .-> P0
    A3 -. degree of minimal poly .-> P0
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Encoding and Decoding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MSG[Message Vector u in Fq power k] --> MUL[Multiply by G]
    MUL --> CW[Codeword c in C subset of Fq power n]
    CW --> NOISE[Noisy Channel]
    NOISE --> REC[Received Vector r equals c plus e]
    REC --> SYN[Compute Syndrome s equals H r transpose]
    SYN --> TABLE[Lookup Table for Error Pattern]
    TABLE --> EST[Estimated Error e hat]
    EST --> COR[Corrected Codeword c hat equals r minus e hat]
    COR --> DEC[Decoded Message]
    G[Generator Matrix G of size k by n] --> MUL
    H[Parity Check Matrix H of size n-k by n] --> SYN
    G -. H G transpose equals 0 .-> H
```

## 4.4 Construction Pipeline: From Base Field to Finite Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Prime p] --> F1[Prime Field Fp = Z mod pZ]
    N[Integer n greater than or equal to 1] --> IRR
    F1 --> IRR[Choose Irreducible p x in Fp x of degree n]
    IRR --> Q[Quotient Ring Fp x mod p x]
    Q --> FF[Finite Field Fp to the n with p to the n elements]
    FF --> VEC[Vector Space of dim n over Fp]
    FF --> MUL[Multiplicative Group cyclic of order p to the n minus 1]
    VEC --> SUB[Subspaces for Codes]
    MUL --> POW[Powers of Frobenius give Conjugates]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
> **Define the minimal polynomial of an algebraic element $\alpha$ over a field $F$. State any three of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The **minimal polynomial** of an algebraic element $\alpha$ over a field $F$, denoted $m_{\alpha}(x)$, is the monic polynomial of least degree in $F[x]$ such that $m_{\alpha}(\alpha) = 0$.

**Properties:**

1. **Uniqueness:** $m_{\alpha}(x)$ is the unique monic polynomial of minimum degree in $F[x]$ having $\alpha$ as a root.
2. **Irreducibility:** $m_{\alpha}(x)$ is irreducible over $F$.
3. **Divisibility:** If $f(x) \in F[x]$ and $f(\alpha) = 0$, then $m_{\alpha}(x)$ divides $f(x)$ in $F[x]$.
4. **Degree Relation:** $\deg(m_{\alpha}(x)) = [F(\alpha) : F]$, the degree of the simple extension.

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
> **Define a field extension. If $F \subseteq K \subseteq L$ are fields, state the Tower Law and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

A **field extension** $K/F$ consists of two fields $F \subseteq K$ such that the field operations of $K$ restrict to those of $F$ on the subset $F$.

**Tower Law:**

$$
[L : F] \;=\; [L : K] \cdot [K : F]
$$

**Significance:** The Tower Law allows us to compute the overall extension degree as a product of intermediate degrees. This is the key tool for computing the degree of composite extensions and is essential when analyzing the structure of finite fields $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ as iterated extensions of the prime field $\mathbb{F}_p$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Construct the finite field $\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$ using the irreducible polynomial $p(x) = x^3 + x + 1 \in \mathbb{F}_2[x]$. List all eight elements and verify that $p(x)$ is irreducible. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find all conjugates of $\alpha$ and $\alpha^3$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$, where $\alpha$ is a root of $p(x) = x^3 + x + 1$. Hence write down the minimal polynomial of $\alpha^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1: Verify irreducibility.**

To check irreducibility of $x^3 + x + 1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$, we test for roots:

$$
p(0) = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1 \neq 0
$$

$$
p(1) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \neq 0
$$

Since a degree-3 polynomial over a field is reducible iff it has a root, and $p(x)$ has no root in $\mathbb{F}_2$, we conclude $p(x)$ is **irreducible** over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

**Step 2: Construct the quotient ring.**

The finite field is:

$$
\mathbb{F}_{2^3} \;=\; \mathbb{F}_2[x] \,/\, \langle x^3 + x + 1 \rangle
$$

Every element is uniquely represented as $a_0 + a_1 \alpha + a_2 \alpha^2$ with $a_i \in \{0, 1\}$, where $\alpha^3 = \alpha + 1$.

**Step 3: List the eight elements.**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{F}_{2^3} \;=\; \{0,\ 1,\ \alpha,\ \alpha+1,\ \alpha^2,\ \alpha^2+1,\ \alpha^2+\alpha,\ \alpha^2+\alpha+1\}\;}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the field has $2^3 = 8$ elements.**

Each coefficient $a_i$ has 2 choices, giving $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ elements. The field axioms are inherited from the quotient ring, which is a field because $\langle x^3 + x + 1 \rangle$ is a maximal ideal (since $x^3 + x + 1$ is irreducible).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Compute conjugates of $\alpha$.**

Using the Frobenius map $\phi(\beta) = \beta^2$ and the relation $\alpha^3 = \alpha + 1$:

$$
\alpha^2 \quad \text{(already distinct from } \alpha\text{)}
$$

$$
\alpha^4 = \alpha \cdot \alpha^3 = \alpha(\alpha + 1) = \alpha^2 + \alpha
$$

**Step 2: Check whether the orbit has closed.**

$$
\alpha^8 = (\alpha^4)^2 = (\alpha^2 + \alpha)^2 = \alpha^4 + \alpha^2 = (\alpha^2 + \alpha) + \alpha^2 = \alpha
$$

So we get the cycle $\alpha \to \alpha^2 \to \alpha^4 = \alpha^2 + \alpha \to \alpha^8 = \alpha$, giving exactly 3 distinct conjugates, matching $\deg m_\alpha = 3$.

**Step 3: Compute conjugates of $\alpha^3 = \alpha + 1$.**

Let $\beta = \alpha + 1$:

$$
\beta^2 = (\alpha + 1)^2 = \alpha^2 + 1
$$

$$
\beta^4 = (\alpha^2 + 1)^2 = \alpha^4 + 1 = (\alpha^2 + \alpha) + 1 = \alpha^2 + \alpha + 1
$$

**Step 4: Check orbit closure for $\beta$.**

$$
\beta^8 = (\beta^4)^2 = (\alpha^2 + \alpha + 1)^2 = \alpha^4 + \alpha^2 + 1 = (\alpha^2 + \alpha) + \alpha^2 + 1 = \alpha + 1 = \beta
$$

So $\beta$ has exactly 3 distinct conjugates.

**Step 5: Write the minimal polynomial of $\beta = \alpha^3$.**

$$
m_{\beta}(x) = (x - \beta)(x - \beta^2)(x - \beta^4)
$$

Since $\alpha$ and $\beta = \alpha + 1 = \alpha^3$ are both primitive elements of $\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$, they share the same minimal polynomial:

$$
\boxed{\;m_{\alpha^3}(x) \;=\; x^3 + x + 1\;}
$$

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Define a vector space $V$ over a field $F$. State and prove the subspace test. Give an example of a non-trivial subspace of $\mathbb{F}_2^3$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Let $C$ be a linear code over $\mathbb{F}_2$ with generator matrix:
>
> $$
> G \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
> $$
>
> Find a parity-check matrix $H$ for $C$ and verify that the dual code $C^\perp$ has dimension $n - k = 2$. List all four codewords of $C$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1: Definition.**

A **vector space** $V$ over a field $F$ is an abelian group $(V, +)$ together with a scalar multiplication map $F \times V \to V$ denoted $(c, v) \mapsto cv$ satisfying:

1. $c(u + v) = cu + cv$ for all $c \in F$, $u, v \in V$ (distributivity over vector addition)
2. $(c + d)v = cv + dv$ for all $c, d \in F$, $v \in V$ (distributivity over scalar addition)
3. $c(dv) = (cd)v$ for all $c, d \in F$, $v \in V$ (associativity)
4. $1 \cdot v = v$ for all $v \in V$ (identity)

**Step 2: Subspace Test.**

**Theorem:** A non-empty subset $W \subseteq V$ is a subspace of $V$ if and only if for all $u, v \in W$ and all $c \in F$:

$$
u + cv \;\in\; W
$$

**Proof:**

($\Rightarrow$) If $W$ is a subspace, it is closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication, so $u + cv \in W$ trivially.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose the condition holds for all $u, v \in W$, $c \in F$.

- Take $c = 0$: $u + 0 \cdot v = u \in W$ (gives nothing new).
- Take $u = v$ and $c = -1$: $v + (-1)v = 0 \in W$. So the zero vector is in $W$.
- Take $u = 0$ and any $c$: $0 + cv = cv \in W$. So $W$ is closed under scalar multiplication.
- Take $c = 1$: $u + 1 \cdot v = u + v \in W$. So $W$ is closed under vector addition.

Thus $W$ is a non-empty subset closed under addition and scalar multiplication, hence a subspace. $\blacksquare$

**Step 3: Example.**

The set $W = \{(0, 0, 0), (1, 1, 0)\}$ is a non-trivial subspace of $\mathbb{F}_2^3$. It is non-trivial because it does not equal $\{0\}$ or $\mathbb{F}_2^3$. It is a subspace because it is closed under addition: $(1, 1, 0) + (1, 1, 0) = (0, 0, 0)$ and addition of the zero vector yields itself.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Determine the code parameters.**

The generator matrix $G$ is a $2 \times 4$ matrix with rank 2 over $\mathbb{F}_2$. Therefore $C$ is a $[4, 2]$ linear code with $2^2 = 4$ codewords.

**Step 2: Find a parity-check matrix $H$.**

A parity-check matrix $H$ of size $(n - k) \times n = 2 \times 4$ must satisfy $H G^T = 0$ (the zero matrix over $\mathbb{F}_2$).

Compute $G^T$:

$$
G^T \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

We need $H = \begin{pmatrix} a & b & c & d \\ e & f & g & h \end{pmatrix}$ with $H G^T = 0$.

From the first row of $H$ dotted with each column of $G^T$:

- $a \cdot 1 + b \cdot 0 + c \cdot 1 + d \cdot 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a + c + d = 0$
- $a \cdot 0 + b \cdot 1 + c \cdot 1 + d \cdot 0 = 0 \Rightarrow b + c = 0$

From the second row of $H$:

- $e + g + h = 0$
- $f + g = 0$

Choose the first parity-check row by setting $c = 1$, then $b = 1$, $a + d = 1$. Pick $a = 1$, $d = 0$:

First row: $(1, 1, 1, 0)$.

For the second row, set $g = 1$, then $f = 1$, $e + h = 1$. Pick $e = 0$, $h = 1$:

Second row: $(0, 1, 1, 1)$.

So:

$$
\boxed{\;H \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\;}
$$

**Step 3: Verify $H G^T = 0$.**

$$
H G^T = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Row 1 times Col 1: $1 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 0$.
Row 1 times Col 2: $0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 0$.
Row 2 times Col 1: $0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 0$.
Row 2 times Col 2: $0 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 0$.

All entries are 0, confirming $H G^T = 0$.

**Step 4: Verify dimension of dual code.**

$C^\perp$ is the row space of $H$, which has dimension equal to $\text{rank}(H) = 2$ (the two rows are linearly independent over $\mathbb{F}_2$). This matches $n - k = 4 - 2 = 2$. $\checkmark$

**Step 5: Enumerate all four codewords of $C$.**

The codewords are $\mathbf{c} = u G$ for $u \in \mathbb{F}_2^2 = \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$:

- $u = 00$: $\mathbf{c} = (0, 0, 0, 0)$
- $u = 10$: $\mathbf{c} = (1, 0, 1, 1)$
- $u = 01$: $\mathbf{c} = (0, 1, 1, 0)$
- $u = 11$: $\mathbf{c} = (1, 1, 0, 1)$

$$
\boxed{\;C \;=\; \{(0,0,0,0),\ (1,0,1,1),\ (0,1,1,0),\ (1,1,0,1)\}\;}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting characteristic-2 squaring:** In $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}$, $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + b^2$ because the cross-term $2ab = 0$. Students often write $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ and leave the $2ab$ term, costing 1 mark per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Missing the field relation:** When computing $\alpha^k$ for $k \geq n$ in $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$, students forget to reduce $\alpha^n$ using $\alpha^n = $ appropriate combination of lower powers. This cascades into wrong conjugates and wrong minimal polynomials.
>
> 3. **Not verifying irreducibility:** KTU examiners explicitly require a proof of irreducibility when constructing a finite field. Merely stating "$p(x)$ is irreducible" without testing for roots or applying Eisenstein's criterion costs up to 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing degree of minimal polynomial with order of element:** The degree $\deg m_\alpha$ counts the number of distinct conjugates, **not** the multiplicative order of $\alpha$. Mixing these two is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Skipping the subspace verification:** When asked "is $W$ a subspace?", students often give the set without checking closure under addition, scalar multiplication, or the presence of $0$. Always show the **subspace test** explicitly.
>
> 6. **Matrix rank computation errors:** For rank over $\mathbb{F}_2$, remember to use $\mathbb{F}_2$-row operations only (no division by scalars other than 1). A row of all zeros must be counted, not deleted silently.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Conjugate elements** are elements that share the same minimal polynomial over the base field.
- The **minimal polynomial** $m_\alpha(x)$ is the monic irreducible polynomial of least degree in $F[x]$ with $\alpha$ as a root; it is **unique**.
- The **degree of the minimal polynomial** equals the **extension degree**: $\deg m_\alpha = [F(\alpha) : F]$.
- The **conjugates of $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$** are exactly $\alpha, \alpha^p, \alpha^{p^2}, \ldots$ until repetition; the number of distinct conjugates is $\deg m_\alpha$.
- The **Tower Law** states $[L : F] = [L : K] \cdot [K : F]$ for $F \subseteq K \subseteq L$.
- A **finite field** $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ is constructed as the quotient ring $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/\langle p(x) \rangle$ where $p(x)$ is an irreducible polynomial of degree $n$ in $\mathbb{F}_p[x]$.
- The **multiplicative group** $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}^\times$ is cyclic of order $p^n - 1$; a generator is called a **primitive element**.
- A **subspace** $W$ of $V$ satisfies the **subspace test**: $u + cv \in W$ for all $u, v \in W$ and $c \in F$.
- The **dual space** $V^* = \text{Hom}_F(V, F)$ has $\dim V^* = \dim V$.
- For a linear code $C$ with generator matrix $G$ and parity-check matrix $H$: $H G^T = 0$, $\dim C = k$, $\dim C^\perp = n - k$.
- **Syndrome decoding** uses $s = H r^T$ to identify the error pattern in a received vector $r$.
- The **Rank-Nullity Theorem** gives $\text{rank}(A) + \text{nullity}(A) = n$ for $A : F^n \to F^m$.
- **Characteristic-2 identity:** $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + b^2$ in any field of characteristic 2 (essential for $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}$ arithmetic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Control Coding - Relevance of Error Control Codes in Communication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT744 - CODING THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Control Coding - Relevance of Error Control Codes in Communication Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Error Control Coding (ECC)** is a branch of digital communications and information theory concerned with the design, analysis, and implementation of codes that enable the **reliable transmission of digital data** over noisy communication channels. It encompasses two fundamental operations:

1. **Encoding** — the systematic addition of controlled redundancy to the information symbols at the transmitter.
2. **Decoding** — the exploitation of this redundancy at the receiver to **detect** and/or **correct** errors introduced by the channel.

Mathematically, an error control code is a mapping from a $k$-dimensional information vector space $\mathbb{F}_q^k$ to an $n$-dimensional codeword space $\mathbb{F}_q^n$ (where $n > k$), forming a code $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{F}_q^n$ with cardinality $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = q^k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Error Control Coding is the cornerstone of Module 2. It establishes *why* redundancy is essential in digital systems, *how* reliability is quantified, and *what* theoretical limits (Shannon bound) govern the ultimate performance of any coding scheme.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Registered Post" Model

Imagine sending a critical legal document through ordinary mail.

- **Without ECC:** You simply drop a one-page letter in the mailbox. If it is lost, smudged, or torn, the recipient receives nothing or receives a corrupted version — the message is gone forever.
- **With ECC:** You send the same document, but you also (a) **photocopy it three times** (redundancy), (b) **number every line** (parity/structure), and (c) **require the receiver to call back and read a specific line** to confirm correctness (acknowledgment).
- The photocopies are **redundant bits** (parity bits, check symbols).
- The numbering system is the **code structure** (block/convolution/cyclic).
- The callback is an **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** mechanism.

If the postman (channel noise) tears one copy, the receiver can still reconstruct the original from the other two. This is exactly what error-correcting codes achieve in digital communication.

### 1.3 Why Do Errors Occur in Communication?

A communication channel is any physical medium (copper wire, optical fibre, RF spectrum, storage media) through which data propagates. Errors are introduced by:

| Error Source | Physical Origin | Typical Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Noise** | Random electron motion (Johnson–Nyquist) | Random single-bit errors |
| **Impulse Noise** | Lightning, switching transients, ESD | **Burst errors** |
| **Fading** | Multipath propagation, shadowing | Long bursts / signal dropouts |
| **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** | Bandwidth-limited channel | Correlated errors |
| **Quantization Noise** | ADC finite precision | Random low-amplitude errors |
| **Crosstalk** | Adjacent channel coupling | Pattern-dependent errors |
| **Storage Defects** | Magnetic media scratches, bit rot | Burst errors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bit Error Rate (BER)** is the primary metric: $\text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total number of transmitted bits}}$. Modern wireless links target BER $\le 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-9}$.

### 1.4 The Two Pillars of Error Control

Error control strategies bifurcate into two principal philosophies:

1. **Forward Error Correction (FEC):** The receiver **autonomously corrects** errors using the embedded redundancy. No retransmission. Used in **simplex**, latency-critical, or broadcast links (satellite TV, deep-space probes, DVDs, 5G control channels).
2. **Automatic Repeat reQuest (ARQ):** The receiver **detects** errors and requests a retransmission via a feedback channel. Used in **half/full-duplex** reliable links (TCP, Wi-Fi data frames, HDLC).

> [!TIP]
> **Hybrid ARQ (HARQ)** combines the best of both worlds: FEC provides baseline correction; ARQ triggers retransmission only when errors exceed the FEC's correction capability. This is the workhorse of **4G/5G mobile broadband**.

### 1.5 Shannon's Revolutionary Insight (1948)

Before 1948, engineers believed that to reduce errors one must either increase transmit power or reduce data rate indefinitely. **Claude E. Shannon** proved the counterintuitive result:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem:** For any discrete memoryless channel, there exists a coding scheme that enables *reliable* (arbitrarily low error probability) communication at any data rate $R < C$, where $C$ is the **channel capacity** (bits/channel use). Conversely, reliable communication at $R > C$ is impossible.

This is the theoretical foundation that justifies the entire field of error control coding.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Digital Communication System — Functional Block Architecture

Every digital communication system, irrespective of its physical layer, follows this canonical architecture:

$$
\text{Source} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Source Encoder} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Channel Encoder} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Modulator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Channel} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Demodulator} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Channel Decoder} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sink}
$$

The **Channel Encoder** is precisely where error control coding lives. The **Channel Decoder** performs its inverse.

### 2.2 Classification of Error Control Codes

Error control codes are classified along three orthogonal axes:

**A. By Structure:**

- **Block Codes:** Information is divided into fixed-size $k$-bit blocks; each block is independently mapped to an $n$-bit codeword. Examples: Hamming codes, BCH, Reed–Solomon, LDPC.
- **Convolutional Codes:** Information is processed as a continuous stream; encoding is a sliding-window linear operation over $K$ constraint length. Examples: Viterbi-decoded codes, turbo codes.
- **Concatenated Codes:** An inner code (often convolutional) is cascaded with an outer code (often Reed–Solomon). Used in deep-space missions (e.g., Voyager, Galileo).

**B. By Function:**

- **Error Detection Codes:** Parity check, CRC, Checksum — only *detect* errors.
- **Error Correction Codes:** Hamming, BCH, RS, Convolutional, Turbo, LDPC, Polar — *correct* errors.
- **Hybrid:** Used in HARQ.

**C. By Linearity:**

- **Linear Codes:** $\mathcal{C}$ forms a vector subspace of $\mathbb{F}_q^n$. Most practical codes.
- **Nonlinear Codes:** e.g., Kerdock, Preparata. Rare; offer slight theoretical gains.

### 2.3 Fundamental Parameters of a Code

For an $(n, k)$ block code over $\mathbb{F}_q$:

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Block length | $n$ | Number of symbols in a codeword |
| Message length | $k$ | Number of information symbols |
| Redundancy / parity symbols | $r$ | $r = n - k$ |
| **Code rate** | $R$ | $R = k/n$ |
| **Minimum Hamming distance** | $d_{\min}$ | Smallest Hamming distance between any two distinct codewords |
| Error detection capability | $e_d$ | $e_d = d_{\min} - 1$ |
| Error correction capability | $e_c$ | $e_c = \lfloor (d_{\min} - 1)/2 \rfloor$ |
| Coding gain (at BER $= 10^{-5}$) | $G_c$ | dB reduction in required $E_b/N_0$ vs. uncoded BPSK |
| Weight enumerator | $A(z)$ | $\sum_{i=0}^{n} A_i z^i$ |

### 2.4 The Three Canonical Channel Models (KTU High-Priority)

**A. Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC):**

The most fundamental channel model. Each transmitted bit is independently flipped with probability $p$, and received correctly with probability $(1-p)$.

$$
P(Y=0 \mid X=1) = P(Y=1 \mid X=0) = p
$$

The mutual information is:
$$
I(X;Y) = 1 - H_2(p)
$$
where $H_2(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$ is the binary entropy function. Channel capacity:
$$
C_{\text{BSC}} = 1 - H_2(p) \quad \text{bits/channel use}
$$

**B. Binary Erasure Channel (BEC):**

Each transmitted bit is erased (received as $?$) with probability $\epsilon$, and received correctly with probability $(1-\epsilon)$. No bit-flips occur — only erasures.

$$
C_{\text{BEC}} = 1 - \epsilon \quad \text{bits/channel use}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The BEC is *easier* to handle than the BSC because erasures reveal their locations. Capacity is reached trivially with rate $R = 1 - \epsilon$ via a Maximum Distance Separable (MDS) code.

**C. Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) Channel:**

Continuous-valued output. The fundamental Shannon–Hartley capacity:

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{E_b R}{N_0}\right) \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

where $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), $S$ is signal power, $N = N_0 B$ is noise power, $E_b$ is energy per information bit, and $N_0/2$ is the noise power spectral density.

### 2.5 Shannon Limit (Theoretical Lower Bound)

Setting $R \to 0$ in the Shannon–Hartley equation, the minimum energy per information bit required for reliable communication is:

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\min} = \ln 2 \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}\;}
$$

This is the **absolute Shannon limit**. No code, regardless of complexity, can communicate reliably below this threshold on the AWGN channel.

### 2.6 ARQ Schemes — Three Flavours

**1. Stop-and-Wait ARQ (SW-ARQ):**
- Sender transmits one frame, then halts.
- Waits for **ACK** (positive) or **NAK** (negative).
- On NAK, retransmits the same frame.
- Throughput:
$$
\eta_{\text{SW}} = \frac{(1-p_f) \cdot k}{k + r + 2t_p \cdot R_b} \cdot \text{(frame efficiency factor)}
$$
where $p_f$ is frame error probability, $t_p$ is one-way propagation delay, $R_b$ is bit rate. Highly inefficient on high-latency links.

**2. Go-Back-N ARQ (GBN-ARQ):**
- Sender pipelines up to $N$ outstanding frames.
- On NAK for frame $i$, retransmits frames $i, i+1, \ldots, i+N-1$.
- Receiver discards out-of-order frames.
- Window size: $W \le 2^k - 1$ (to avoid ambiguity).

**3. Selective Repeat ARQ (SR-ARQ):**
- Sender pipelines $N$ frames.
- On NAK, retransmits **only** the erroneous frame.
- Receiver buffers out-of-order frames for reordering.
- Window size: $W \le 2^{k-1}$ for half-duplex.
- Highest throughput; highest receiver complexity.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Notes / Units} \\
\hline
\text{Code rate} & R = k/n & \text{dimensionless, } 0 < R \le 1 \\
\hline
\text{Coding efficiency} & \eta_c = R & \text{same as code rate} \\
\hline
\text{Hamming distance} & d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_i \mathbf{1}(x_i \ne y_i) & \text{number of disagreeing positions} \\
\hline
\text{Hamming weight} & w(\mathbf{x}) = d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{0}) & \text{nonzero entries} \\
\hline
\text{Error detection bound} & e_d = d_{\min} - 1 & \text{maximum detectable errors} \\
\hline
\text{Error correction bound} & e_c = \lfloor (d_{\min} - 1)/2 \rfloor & \text{maximum correctable errors} \\
\hline
\text{Binary entropy} & H_2(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p) & \text{bits} \\
\hline
\text{BSC capacity} & C_{\text{BSC}} = 1 - H_2(p) & \text{bits/channel use} \\
\hline
\text{BEC capacity} & C_{\text{BEC}} = 1 - \epsilon & \text{bits/channel use} \\
\hline
\text{Shannon-Hartley} & C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) & \text{bits/second} \\
\hline
\text{Shannon limit} & (E_b/N_0)_{\min} = \ln 2 \approx -1.59 \text{ dB} & \text{AWGN} \\
\hline
\text{Singleton bound} & d_{\min} \le n - k + 1 & \text{MDS codes attain equality} \\
\hline
\text{Hamming bound} & \sum_{i=0}^{e_c} \binom{n}{i} \le 2^{n-k} & \text{perfect codes attain equality} \\
\hline
\text{Plotkin bound} & d_{\min} \le \frac{2nk}{n(k-1)+k} & \text{large-distance regime} \\
\hline
\text{Sphere-packing prob.} & P_e \le 2^{-n(E_r(R))} & E_r(R): \text{Gallager random-coding exponent} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Error control coding underpins **every** modern digital system: 4G/5G NR (LDPC + Polar), Wi-Fi 6 (LDPC), deep-space communication (RS + convolutional concatenated), QR codes (Reed–Solomon), SSDs/Flash memory (BCH/LDPC), DVB-T2 (LDPC + BCH), and DNA storage (Reed–Solomon). Without ECC, the digital age as we know it would be impossible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Channel Capacity of the Binary Symmetric Channel

We seek the maximum mutual information $I(X;Y)$ over all input distributions $P(X)$ on $\{0,1\}$.

**Step 1.** By symmetry of the BSC (input and output alphabets identical, channel symmetric), $I(X;Y)$ is maximized when the input is **uniform**: $P(X=0) = P(X=1) = 1/2$.

**Step 2.** Compute $H(Y)$ under the uniform input. By symmetry, $P(Y=0) = P(Y=1) = 1/2$, so $H(Y) = 1$ bit.

**Step 3.** Compute the conditional entropy $H(Y \mid X)$. By the channel symmetry, this depends only on $p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(Y \mid X) &= -\sum_{x \in \{0,1\}} P(X=x) \sum_{y \in \{0,1\}} P(Y=y \mid X=x) \log_2 P(Y=y \mid X=x) \\[2mm]
&= -\sum_{x} \frac{1}{2} \Big[ (1-p)\log_2(1-p) + p\log_2 p \Big] \\[2mm]
&= -(1-p)\log_2(1-p) - p\log_2 p \\[2mm]
&= H_2(p)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Apply the chain rule of mutual information:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X;Y) &= H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) \\[1mm]
&= 1 - H_2(p) \quad \text{bits/channel use}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Conclusion: $C_{\text{BSC}} = 1 - H_2(p)$ bits/use. For $p = 0$ (noiseless), $C = 1$. For $p = 1/2$ (pure noise), $C = 0$. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Limit on the AWGN Channel

We start from the Shannon–Hartley equation and express $S$ in terms of $E_b$ and $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{E_b \cdot R_b}{T_b} = E_b \cdot R_b \cdot R \quad \text{(since data rate } R_b = R/T_b \text{ at rate } R \text{ bits/symbol)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — for a *continuous-time* channel, the data rate is $R_b = R \cdot B$ (when using $2B$ real dimensions per second at Nyquist), so $S = E_b \cdot R_b = E_b \cdot R \cdot B$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{E_b R B}{N_0 B}\right) = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{E_b R}{N_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Reliable communication requires $R_b \le C$, i.e. $R \cdot B \le C$, but rate-normalised, $R \le C/B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &\le \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{E_b R}{N_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Exponentiating both sides (base 2):

$$
2^R = 1 + \frac{E_b R}{N_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2^R - 1}{R}
$$

Taking the limit as $R \to 0$ (minimum energy per bit):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\min} &= \lim_{R \to 0} \frac{2^R - 1}{R} = \left.\frac{d}{dR}(2^R - 1)\right\vert_{R=0} \quad \text{(L'Hôpital)} \\[1mm]
&= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \quad \text{(linear)} \\[1mm]
&= 10 \log_{10}(\ln 2) \approx -1.5917 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **Shannon limit** is $\boxed{(E_b/N_0)_{\min} = \ln 2 \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}}$ for the AWGN channel with BPSK-like input.

### 3.3 Derivation: Go-Back-N ARQ Throughput

Consider a Go-Back-N system with frame length $n_f$ bits, bit rate $R_b$ bps, one-way propagation delay $t_p$, and frame error probability $P_F$.

**Step 1.** Frame transmission time: $t_f = n_f / R_b$.

**Step 2.** Probability a frame is received correctly: $P_c = 1 - P_F$.

**Step 3.** Expected number of transmissions per successful delivery: On a BSC with bit error rate $p$, $P_F = 1 - (1-p)^{n_f}$.

**Step 4.** For GBN-ARQ, every retransmission resends $N$ frames (the erroneous one and the $N-1$ subsequent). The expected cycle involves transmitting $N$ new frames plus, for each failure, an extra $N$ retransmissions.

A more direct derivation: in steady state, the throughput efficiency is

$$
\eta_{\text{GBN}} = \frac{1 - P_F}{1 + 2a} \cdot \left[1 - P_F \cdot (1 - P_F)^{N-1}\right]
$$

where $a = t_p / t_f$ is the normalised propagation delay.

For small $a$ (low-latency) and $P_F \ll 1$:

$$
\eta_{\text{GBN}} \approx \frac{1 - P_F}{1 + 2a}
$$

**Step 5.** Comparison: SR-ARQ achieves

$$
\eta_{\text{SR}} = \frac{1 - P_F}{1 + 2a}
$$

always (each frame is retransmitted independently), so SR-ARQ is **always** at least as good as GBN-ARQ for the same window.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Hamming Bound Application

**Problem:** Show that a binary $(7,4)$ code cannot correct 2 errors, and determine the actual $e_c$.

**Step 1.** Hamming bound: $\sum_{i=0}^{e_c} \binom{n}{i} \le 2^{n-k}$.

For $n=7, k=4, e_c=2$:

$$
\binom{7}{0} + \binom{7}{1} + \binom{7}{2} = 1 + 7 + 21 = 29
$$
$$
2^{n-k} = 2^3 = 8
$$

Since $29 \not\le 8$, a 2-error-correcting code with these parameters is **impossible**.

**Step 2.** Try $e_c = 1$:

$$
\binom{7}{0} + \binom{7}{1} = 1 + 7 = 8 \le 8 \quad \checkmark
$$

Equality holds, so the $(7,4)$ Hamming code is a **perfect code** with $e_c = 1$.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Coding Gain of a Rate-1/2 Convolutional Code

A rate-$1/2$, constraint-length-$7$ convolutional code (the NASA-standard code used in Voyager) achieves a BER of $10^{-5}$ at $E_b/N_0 = 4.5$ dB with Viterbi decoding (hard decision, 3-bit quantisation). Uncoded BPSK requires $E_b/N_0 = 9.6$ dB for the same BER.

**Step 1.** Coding gain:

$$
G_c = \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{uncoded}} - \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{coded}} = 9.6 - 4.5 = 5.1 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2.** Effective $E_b/N_0$ per transmitted bit: since $R = 1/2$, $E_s = E_b/2$ (per channel bit), so the $E_s/N_0$ is $1.5$ dB.

**Step 3.** This $5.1$ dB gain allowed the Voyager mission to communicate across 15 billion km of interstellar space with a 23 W transmitter — impossible without ECC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Digital Communication System with Error Control

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Information Source"] --> se["Source Encoder Compress"]
    se --> ce["Channel Encoder Add Redundancy"]
    ce --> mod["Modulator BPSK QAM OFDM"]
    mod --> ch["Channel Noise Fading ISI"]
    ch --> dem["Demodulator Hard Soft Decisions"]
    dem --> cd["Channel Decoder Correct Errors"]
    cd --> sd["Source Decoder"]
    sd --> sink["Information Sink"]
    ce -.feedback path.-> ack["ACK NAK Channel"]
    ack -.retransmit.-> ce
```

### 4.2 ARQ Protocol State Diagram (Stop-and-Wait)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Transmit: Send Frame
    Transmit --> WaitACK: Timer Starts
    WaitACK --> AckReceived: Receive ACK
```
